revision.text:
[[Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg|285px|thumb|Tanzan Shrine]] The '''Tanzan Shrine''' or '''Danzan Shrine''', also known as the '''Tonomine Temple''', is a [[Buddhist]] temple in [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]]. It was major Buddhist site prior to the anti-Buddhist movement of the [[Meiji Era]], which led to the destruction of many Buddhist temples in Japan. The original temple, a 13 storey wooden pagoda, was built in [[678]]. The present pagoda was built in [[1532]].
''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'' ("Kick-ball Festival") is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play an form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other.
{{japan-struct-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}
<gallery> Image:Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|The grounds of the shrine. Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]] at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|Kemari Matsuri at the shrine </gallery>
[[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:7th century architecture]] [[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Religious building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Buddhism stubs]]

[[Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg|285px|thumb|Tanzan Shrine]] The '''Tanzan Shrine''' or '''Danzan Shrine''', also known as the '''Tonomine Temple''', is a [[Buddhist]] temple in [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]]. It was major Buddhist site prior to the anti-Buddhist movement of the [[Meiji Era]], which led to the destruction of many Buddhist temples in Japan. The original temple, a 13 storey wooden pagoda, was built in [[678]]. The present pagoda was built in [[1532]].
''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'' ("Kick-ball Festival") is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play an form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|The grounds of the shrine. Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]] at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|Kemari Matsuri at the shrine </gallery>
[[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:7th century architecture]] [[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Religious building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Buddhism stubs]]

{{unreferenced|date=July 2008}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = | type = | dedication = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = | website = }} The '''Tanzan Shrine''' or '''Danzan Shrine''', also known as the '''Tōnomine Temple''', is a Shinto shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]]. It was a major Buddhist site before the anti-Buddhist movement of the [[Meiji Era]], which led to the destruction of many Buddhist temples in Japan. The original temple, a 13-story wooden pagoda, was built in [[678]]. The present pagoda was built in [[1532]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'' ("Kick-ball Festival") is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. {{japan-struct-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|The grounds of the shrine. Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]] at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|Kemari Matsuri at the shrine {{coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:670s architecture]] [[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Religious building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Buddhism stubs]] [[de:Tanzan-Schrein]] [[ja:談山神社]]

{{unreferenced|date=July 2008}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = | type = | dedication = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''||談山神社|''Tanzan-jinja''}}, also known as the '''Tōnomine Temple''' (多武峯寺), is a Shinto shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]]. It was a major Buddhist site before the anti-Buddhist movement of the [[Meiji Era]], which led to the destruction of many Buddhist temples in Japan. The original temple, a 13-story wooden pagoda, was built in [[678]]. The present pagoda was built in [[1532]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'' ("Kick-ball Festival") is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Pagoda at Tanzan Shrine Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. 〒633-0032 | phone = 0744-49-0001 | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Tōnomine Temple''' (多武峯寺), is a Shinto shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]]. The original temple, a 13-story wooden pagoda, was built in [[678]]. The present pagoda was built in [[1532]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'' ("Kick-ball Festival") is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == External links == {{Commons|Category:Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:670s architecture]] [[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Religious building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Buddhism stubs]] {{japan-struct-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[de:Tanzan-Schrein]] [[ja:談山神社]]

It was a major Buddhist{{Citation needed|date=November 2010}} site before the anti-Buddhist movement of the [[Meiji Era]], which led to the destruction of many Buddhist temples in Japan. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = Pagoda of Tanzan Shrine | type = | dedication = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = [[Jo'e]] | priest = | address = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]]
〒633-0032 | phone = 0744-49-0001 | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | dlc = 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044}} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036}} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakafu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''shinbutsu bunri'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1858 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:670s architecture]] [[de:Tanzan-Schrein]] [[fr:Tanzan-jinja]] [[ja:談山神社]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = Pagoda of Tanzan Shrine | type = | dedication = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = [[Jo'e]] | priest = | address = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]]
〒633-0032 | phone = 0744-49-0001 | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | dlc = 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044}} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036}} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakafu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1858 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:670s architecture]] [[de:Tanzan-Schrein]] [[fr:Tanzan-jinja]] [[ja:談山神社]]

〒633-0032 | phone = 0744-49-0001 | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tanzan Shrine | dlc = 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044}} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036}} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:670s architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Jo'e | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036}} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:7th-century religious buildings]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

〒633-0032 | phone = 0744-49-0001 | website = http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tanzan Shrine | id=dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044}} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Jo'e | oclc = 683276033}}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036}} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] [[Category:7th-century religious buildings]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] [[Category:7th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = Pagoda of Tanzan Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded_by = [[Jo'e]] | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]]
〒633-0032 | website = {{URL|http://www.tanzan.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, {{Nihongo|Myōraku-ji||}} and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] [[Category:7th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]]

archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:7th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]]

archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakurai, Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = Pagoda of Tanzan Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded_by = [[Jo'e]] | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]]
〒633-0032 | website = {{URL|http://www.tanzan.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]]

The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakurai, Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tanzan Shrine
談山神社 | image = TanzanJinja.JPG | caption = Pagoda of Tanzan Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|57|N|135|51|42|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] | founded_by = [[Jo'e]] | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 319 Tōnomine, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]]
〒633-0032 | website = {{URL|http://www.tanzan.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk [[Jo'e]] (643 – 666). Jo'e was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Jo'e located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). Jo'e moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Jo'e | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja03s3200.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

〒633-0032 | website = {{URL|http://www.tanzan.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tanzan Shrine'''|談山神社|Tanzan-jinja|}}, also known as the '''Danzan Shrine''', the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Shrine'''|多武峯社|Tōnomine-sha}} and the {{Nihongo|'''Tōnomine Temple'''|多武峯寺|Tōnomine-ji}}, is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | oclc = 153301537 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei | title = 談山神社 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tanzan Shrine | id = dlc 2009238904 | oclc = 173191044 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} It is located 5km from [[Ishibutai Kofun]].{{Cite web |title=Google Maps |url=https://www.google.com/maps/dir/'34.466861,135.826139'/34.465833,135.861667/@34.464802,135.8394253,15.5z/data=!4m7!4m6!1m3!2m2!1d135.826139!2d34.466861!1m0!3e2?hl=en&entry=ttu |access-date=2023-10-26 |website=Google Maps |language=en}} ==History== The shrine traces its origin to a [[Tendai]] temple built in the [[Asuka period]] (538 – 710) called Tōnomine-ji, built by the monk {{ill|Jo'e|ja|定恵}} (643 – 666). {{ill|Jo'e|ja|定恵}} was the oldest son of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]] (614 – 669), founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. {{ill|Jo'e|ja|定恵}} located the temple on [[Tōnomine]], a peak of on the southern side of [[Mount Goharetsu]] ({{Convert|619|m|ft}}). {{ill|Jo'e|ja|定恵}} moved the remains of Kamatari to a 13-story pagoda on the site. During the [[Heian period]], the temple developed together with the prosperity of the Fujiwara clan. The emperors [[Emperor Daigo|Daigo]] (884 – 930) and [[Emperor Go-Hanazono|Go-Hanazono]] (1419 – 1471) attached special reverence to the temple, and bestowed it with various honorifics.{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 定恵 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-05 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = {{ill|Jo'e|ja|定恵}} | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 多武峰 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-09-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Tōnomine | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }} Under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'', a system of [[syncretism]] of [[Buddhism]] and ''[[kami]]'' worship, the site was both a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple. The Tanzan Shrine and Tōnomine-ji coexisted on the same site. Tōnomine-ji had two subtemples located within its precincts, Myōraku-ji (妙楽寺) and {{Nihongo|Shōryō-in|聖霊院||}}. The shrine received significant financial support from the [[Tokugawa bakufu]] during the [[Edo period]] (1603 – 1868). During the anti-Buddhist ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' movement after the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 Tanzan Shrine was designated solely as a Shinto shrine dedicated to the worship of the ''kami'' of Fujiwara no Kamatari. The Buddhist structures of the shrine were rededicated as Shinto structures. Under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the Tanzan Shrine was designated a ''bekkaku kanreisha'' in 1874, an Imperial shrine of special status. The shrine lost this designation after the abolition of the ranked shrine system after [[World War II]]. ==Structures== The present thirteen-story wooden [[pagoda]] was built in 1532, and is a reconstruction of the structure built by Jo'e in the Asuka Period. The pagoda is designated an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]. The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is built in the ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' style. It is dedicated to Fujiwara no Kamatari. ==Kemari Matsuri== A ''[[Kemari]] [[Matsuri]]'', or [[kickball]] festival, is held every year on April 29 and the second Sunday in November. On this day, people in ancient costumes and arranged in a circle play a form of [[football]] in which they kick a ball made of deerskin to each other. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Gallery== Image:Tanzan Shrine 3.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja04s3200.jpg|Thirteen-storied pagoda Image:Tanzan jinja pagoda from below.jpg Image:Tanzan jinja01s3200.jpg|Lanterns Image:Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|A secondary building at the shrine. Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 1.jpg|''[[Kemari]]'' ''[[Matsuri]]'' at the shrine Image:Kemari Matsuri at Tanzan Shrine 2.jpg|''Kemari Matsuri'' at the shrine == External links == {{Commons category|Tanzan-jinja}} *[http://www.tanzan.or.jp/ Official Site (Japanese)] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 7th century]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
